fallacies.name: Straw Man
fallacies.description: Misrepresenting an opponent's position to make it easier to attack.
fallacies.example: So you're saying we should just let total chaos reign? Bold.
fallacies.counter: I never claimed that — here's what I actually said.

fallacies.name: Ad Hominem
fallacies.description: Attacking the person instead of their argument.
fallacies.example: You'd believe anything — you failed logic class, remember?
fallacies.counter: My grades aren't the claim on the table. Address the claim.

fallacies.name: Slippery Slope
fallacies.description: Asserting one small step inevitably leads to a catastrophic chain.
fallacies.example: Allow this once and civilization itself unravels by Tuesday.
fallacies.counter: Name the mechanism. Each step needs its own evidence.

fallacies.name: False Dilemma
fallacies.description: Framing a many-optioned question as only two choices.
fallacies.example: Either you agree with me completely, or you're the enemy.
fallacies.counter: That's a false choice — there's a whole spectrum between.

fallacies.name: Circular Reasoning
fallacies.description: Using the conclusion as one of its own premises.
fallacies.example: It's true because I said so, and I'm right because it's true.
fallacies.counter: That just restates the claim — where's the independent support?

fallacies.name: Appeal to False Authority
fallacies.description: Citing an authority with no real expertise on the matter.
fallacies.example: A very famous actor agrees with me, so the science is settled.
fallacies.counter: Fame isn't expertise. What do the people who study this say?

fallacies.name: Hasty Generalization
fallacies.description: Drawing a sweeping conclusion from too few examples.
fallacies.example: Two people I met were rude, so everyone from there is rude.
fallacies.counter: Two isn't a sample — it's an anecdote. Broaden the evidence.

fallacies.name: Red Herring
fallacies.description: Introducing an irrelevant point to derail the argument.
fallacies.example: Sure, but have you considered how nice the weather is today?
fallacies.counter: Interesting, but irrelevant. Back to the actual point.

fallacies.name: Appeal to Emotion
fallacies.description: Manipulating feelings in place of a valid argument.
fallacies.example: If you had any heart at all, you'd already agree with me.
fallacies.counter: My feelings aren't evidence. Give me a reason, not a guilt trip.

fallacies.name: Tu Quoque
fallacies.description: Deflecting criticism by accusing the critic of hypocrisy.
fallacies.example: Oh, so YOU'VE never once been wrong? Then sit down.
fallacies.counter: Whether I'm perfect has nothing to do with whether you're right.

fallacies.name: Bandwagon
fallacies.description: Claiming something is true because it's popular.
fallacies.example: Everybody already believes this, so you're just behind.
fallacies.counter: Popularity isn't proof. Millions can share the same mistake.

fallacies.name: No True Scotsman
fallacies.description: Redefining a category on the fly to dodge a counterexample.
fallacies.example: No REAL expert would ever disagree with me, by definition.
fallacies.counter: You're just moving the goalposts to erase the exception.

fallacies.name: Appeal to Ignorance
fallacies.description: Treating a lack of disproof as proof.
fallacies.example: No one has proven me wrong, so I must be right.
fallacies.counter: Absence of a rebuttal isn't evidence. The burden is on you.

fallacies.name: Equivocation
fallacies.description: Sliding between two meanings of the same word mid-argument.
fallacies.example: A feather is light, and light travels fast — so feathers are quick!
fallacies.counter: You switched the meaning of the word halfway through.

fallacies.name: Post Hoc
fallacies.description: Assuming that because B followed A, A caused B.
fallacies.example: I wore red and we won, so my shirt clearly won the game.
fallacies.counter: Sequence isn't causation. Correlation needs a mechanism.
